Footer: Error-budget-used = observed breach rate / allowed rate; > 100% means budget overspent. H-07 strip: within-budget / over-budget per cell with budget %. H-08 — Tail Risk Analysis . | Fault Category | Metric | IQM | P95 | CVaR₉₅ | CVaR / IQM ratio | Risk level |—|—|—|—|—|—|—| . Footer: CVaR/IQM bands: < 1.5 mild · 1.5–2.0 moderate · ≥ 2.0 significant. H-08 strip: risk-level summary per category. H-09 — Temporal Stability . | Fault Category | Metric | CUSUM Alarm | EWMA Trend (λ = 0.2) | EWMA Alarm | Verdict |—|—|—|—|—|—| . Footer: CUSUM threshold h = 5σ; EWMA λ = 0.2 with ±3σ control limits. Verdict STABLE if neither fires; DRIFT_DETECTED otherwise. H-09 strip: STABLE / DRIFT_DETECTED summary, with the explicit takeaway that observed SLA failures (if any) reflect a capability ceiling, not regression over time.

293.title: Part II — Fault Injection Analysis
293.content: A divider strip introduces Part II with the label Part II and title Fault Injection Analysis.

294.title: 11. Fault Category Analysis
294.content: One sub-section per category. Each sub-section presents a header card with key metrics, then four narrative blocks synthesized by the LLM Council. Per-category header card (one row of pills): . Fault: {fault_name} · Runs: {n} · Detection: {%} · Mitigation: {%} · Reasoning: {x}/1.0 . Four narrative blocks (each a small card with a vertical accent bar): . | Agent Summary — overall behavior across runs (no severity label; Confidence + Agreement only). | Response & Reasoning Quality — Assessment + Confidence + Agreement + paragraph. | Security Compliance — Assessment + Confidence + Agreement + paragraph. | RAI Compliance — Assessment + Confidence + Agreement + paragraph. | . Each block displays: . | Field | Source | . | Assessment | LLM Council severity label (Strong / Adequate / Weak / —) | . | Confidence | LLM Council confidence (High / Medium / Low) | . | Agreement | Inter-judge agreement (0.0 – 1.0) | . | Body | Multi-sentence narrative synthesized by the meta-reconciler | . Categories are separated by a thin horizontal rule.

295.title: 12. Known Limitations
295.content: Ranked list. Each entry is a card with a left accent bar, severity tag, scope tag, and optional taxonomy tag. Header narrative: . Known limitations are identified through aggregated analysis across all certification runs by an independent LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and annotated with the frequency of occurrence. Items are sorted by severity — highest impact first. Per-limitation card fields: . | Field | Format | . | Rank | L1., L2., … | . | Severity | [High] / [Medium] / [Low] | . | Scope | (Cross-cutting) or ({category}) | . | Taxonomy tag | optional pill: Statistical Inference / Data Quality / etc. | . | Frequency | k/n runs (%) (omitted when scope = Cross-cutting) | . | Body | One-sentence description | . Note. Statistical-inference limitations (e.g., “detection capability is statistically uncertifiable, Wilson CI lower bound 0% on Network”) must carry the Statistical Inference tag and cite the originating hypothesis.

296.title: 13. Recommendations
296.content: Same card style as Section 12, but priority-tagged. Header narrative: . Recommendations are generated by the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings across all certification runs. Each recommendation is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and linked to the originating fault category. Per-recommendation card fields: . | Field | Format | . | Rank | R1., R2., … | . | Priority | [Critical] / [High] / [Medium] / [Low] | . | Scope | (Cross-cutting) or ({category}) | . | Taxonomy tag | optional pill: Statistical Inference / Data Quality / etc. | . | Body | One-sentence actionable directive | . Note. Recommendations driven by statistical inference (e.g., “treat detection improvement as a capability-engineering effort — H-09 shows performance is stable across runs; collecting more runs will not raise rates”) must carry the Statistical Inference tag.

297.title: Visualization Selection Quick Reference
297.content: | Data Type | Descriptive (left) | Inferential (right) | Avoid | . | Timing distributions (TTD, TTM) | Bar chart — Median + P95 per category | BCa 95% CI strip on IQM | Pie chart, line chart | . | Quality scores (reasoning, response) | Grouped bar (0–10 scale) | BCa 95% CI strip on IQM, zoomed scale + threshold line | 3D charts | . | Success / compliance rates | Grouped bar with threshold line | Wilson 95% CI brackets per category | Pie chart | . | Hallucination / safety counts | Bar (mean) with max marker | One-sided Wilson upper bound | Bar without max | . | Status fields (RAI, security) | Grouped bar (rates) | Wilson CI strip with target line | Bar chart for binary status only | . | Token counts | Stacked bar (input + output) | — (no inferential pair) | Scatter plot | . | Overall agent profile | Radar chart with legend | — | Multiple disconnected KPIs | . | Cross-category effect | — | A₁₂ + Holm-corrected MWU table | Single combined chart | . | Drift / time-series | Run-ordered scatter | CUSUM + EWMA control chart | Bar chart per run | .

298.title: Hypothesis Strip Format
298.content: Every quantitative section closes with a one-line strip carrying the verdict. H-0X {Hypothesis name} {key metric 1} · {key metric 2} · {key metric 3} Statistical findings: {one-paragraph narrative; bold/italic the headline numbers} . Status classes: . | Class | When | Visual cue | . | pass | All targets / thresholds cleared | green-tinted facts | . | conditional | CI straddles threshold; insufficient evidence | indigo (neutral) | . | flag | Threshold breached / null hypothesis rejected against the agent | amber-tinted facts | .

299.title: Anti-Patterns to Avoid
299.content: | Anti-Pattern | Why It Fails | Do This Instead | . | Reporting only point estimates (mean / rate) | Hides uncertainty; an n = 5 success rate of 100% is meaningless | Always pair with the CI from the inferential plot | . | Aggregating across categories when H-03 / H-05 reject equality | Single mean misrepresents at least one category | Report per-category Wilson / BCa intervals | . | Treating a flag as a sample-size problem when H-09 shows stability | Drives wasted re-runs instead of capability work | Cite H-09 when recommending engineering changes vs more runs | . | Dropping the run count from rate displays | A 100% rate on n = 3 means nothing | Show k/n alongside every rate | . | Long paragraphs for descriptive metrics | Numbers get lost in prose | Tables first; one-line interpretation if needed | . | Pie charts for category comparison | Cannot compare slices across multiple pies | Grouped bar chart or stacked bar | . | Mixing scales on one axis | Misleading visual comparison | Normalize to 0–1 or use dual axes with explicit labels | . | Dumping raw LLM Council output | Verbose, inconsistent formatting | Use the structured per-category card (Assessment / Confidence / Agreement / body) | . | Omitting the Statistical Inference tag on stats-driven limitations / recommendations | Reader can’t tell which findings are inferential vs descriptive | Tag explicitly and cite the originating hypothesis (H-0X) | .

300.title: 5.3 — Certification Decision Guide
300.content: When you run the AgentCert pipeline, which output you get depends on how much data you have and whether Phase E (hypothesis testing) ran. This page explains what each outcome looks like and what you should do about it. For the full scenario definitions and conditions, see 2.6 Certification Scenarios.

301.title: The Four Scenarios at a Glance
301.content: | Scenario | What Triggered It | What You Get | . | S1 — Full Certification | ≥ 30 runs per fault category + Phase E ran | 12-section report with statistical appendix (A1–A5) | . | S2 — Certification, No Stats | 1–29 runs per fault category | 12-section report with explicit “statistical inadequacy” notice where hypothesis blocks would appear | . | S3 — No Certificate | Every run failed before producing usable telemetry | No report. Upstream failure. Check your trace pipeline. | . | S4 — Cert with Warnings | Runs completed but Phase 1 extracted no usable metrics | Report exists but key sections show “data unavailable.” Something went wrong in extraction. | .

302.title: What to Do in Each Case

303.title: S1 — Full Certification
303.content: This is the definitive output. The report includes confidence intervals, hypothesis test results, and a certified lower-bound floor for detection and mitigation rates. This is the version suitable for handing to a customer or submitting for sign-off. Check the statistical appendix (Section 12) to confirm hypothesis tests passed and effect sizes are meaningful, not just statistically significant.

304.title: S2 — Certification, Aggregation Only
304.content: You have a real report — mean/median performance, detection rates, qualitative narratives — but the statistics are directional, not certified. The right response depends on your customer’s requirements: . | If they need certified lower bounds, run more experiments to reach n ≥ 30 and re-run Phase E | If the directional data is sufficient for their purposes, S2 is acceptable for a preliminary review | . The “statistical inadequacy” notices in the report are explicit — the reader knows what’s missing without any risk of misinterpretation.

305.title: S3 — All Runs Failed
305.content: Nothing reached Phase 1. This is an infrastructure or trace collection problem, not an agent problem. Check: . | Was Langfuse receiving telemetry during the experiment? | Did the trace file get generated at all? | Are the trace spans tagged with fault context? | . Don’t re-run certification until you’ve confirmed the trace pipeline is healthy. See 2.5 Trace Collection and Preprocessing for what a valid trace looks like.

306.title: S4 — Runs Completed, No Metrics Extracted
306.content: Traces exist but the metrics extractor (Phase 1) produced nothing usable. Common causes: . | Trace spans are missing the expected span types (GENERATION, TOOL_CALL) | Fault injection timestamps are absent from the trace, so TTD/TTM can’t be computed | The fault bucket is empty — Phase 0 couldn’t separate the interleaved events | . A partial report may be emitted with “data unavailable” in the affected sections. Review the Phase 0 bucketing manifest to see what was classified and what wasn’t.

307.title: Choosing When to Stop Running Experiments
307.content: If you’re midway through a campaign and asking “do I have enough runs?”: . | < 5 runs: Results are directional only. Don’t draw conclusions — you’re in INSUFFICIENT confidence tier. | 5–19 runs: MEDIUM confidence. Useful for internal assessment; not ready for formal certification. | ≥ 20 runs: HIGH confidence. Certification-grade for S2 scenarios. | ≥ 30 runs: Required for Phase E and the S1 full statistical certification. | . Phase E (hypothesis testing) requires at least 30 runs per fault category. If you have 30 runs for some categories but not others, Phase E will run for the categories that qualify and be skipped for the rest.

310.title: Scenarios Injected
310.content: | Fault Type | Target Service | Runs | . | pod-cpu-hog | carts pod | 10 | . | pod-memory-hog | carts pod | 10 | . | pod-network-loss | user pod | 10 | .

311.title: Detection Rate
311.content: | Fault Type | Detected | Rate | . | pod-cpu-hog | 9/10 | 90% | . | pod-memory-hog | 8/10 | 80% | . | pod-network-loss | 0/10 | 0% | .

312.title: Time-to-Detect (where detected)
312.content: | Fault Type | Mean TTD | Median TTD | Range | . | pod-cpu-hog | 5.8s | 4.5s | 1s – 13s | . | pod-memory-hog | 35s | 42s | 0.4s – 59s | . | pod-network-loss | — | — | — (not detected) | .

313.title: Time-to-Mitigate
313.content: No mitigation was recorded for any fault across all 30 runs. The agent operated in observation/diagnosis mode only.

314.title: Observations
314.content: . | CPU hog is detected quickly and reliably: 90% detection rate, most runs under 7s. The agent identifies CPU pressure via pods_top and PromQL queries. | Memory hog detection is slow and variable: 80% rate but TTD spans 0.4s–59s, indicating inconsistent detection paths. | Network loss is a complete blind spot: 0/10 runs detected. Later resolved by removing the input field from spans to reduce token overhead. | No mitigation coverage: TTM is null across the board — remediation actions were not wired up or not captured. | .

315.title: Part B — Pipeline Validation Scenarios (notebook: metric_scorecard_pipeline_executed.ipynb)
315.content: These synthetic scenarios were used to validate the TTD/TTM scoring pipeline before applying it to real data. Each scenario tests a different edge case in the scoring logic. Fault types across all scenarios: pod-cpu-hog, pod-memory-hog, pod-network-loss SLA thresholds used: pod-cpu-hog = 120s, pod-memory-hog = 90s, pod-network-loss = 180s . | # | Scenario | Runs | What it tests | . | 1 | Normal — all detects, within SLA | 30 | Baseline: all VALID, all compliant. Cumulative score ~0.59. | . | 2 | Null-heavy (~70% MISSING) | 30 | High miss rate. Score drops to ~0.15 — detection-weighting penalizes misses correctly. | . | 3 | Low variance (TTD ~40% of SLA) | 30 | Tight cluster, no outliers. Score ~0.64, no quality flags. | . | 4 | SLA breach (some TTD > 1.5× SLA) | 30 | Mixed: some within SLA, some severe breaches. Score collapses to ~0.04. | . | 5 | Small sample (n=5) | 5 | Low confidence tier. mixed_category_health flag triggered due to category score spread. | . | 6 | Category imbalance | 30 | Resource category healthy; network category null-heavy. mixed_category_health flag. | . | 7a | Small N baseline (n=3, all good) | 3 | All within SLA. Score ~0.66, no flags. | . | 7b | Small N with SLA breach (n=3, one breach) | 3 | Last run breaches at 1.5× SLA. Score drops to 0.47, skewed_distribution flag triggered. | .

316.title: Real TTM Data (12-05-26-sequential-aarya-30run)
316.content: A separate real dataset (ttm_by_run_category_fault.json) with 31 runs was tested against the TTM metric. Result: 0% mitigation rate — all 93 observations are MISSING. Same fault types (pod-cpu-hog, pod-memory-hog, pod-network-loss) and same SLA thresholds. Confirms that mitigation tracking was not functional in the sequential-aarya experiment.

317.title: Observations
317.content: . | Detection-weighted scoring works as intended: Null-heavy scenario correctly produces a near-zero cumulative score despite some valid detections — the multiplier encodes both “how often” and “how well”. | SLA breach scoring is appropriately punishing: A few severe breaches (>2× SLA) drive the score to near zero even when some runs pass, which is the desired safety-conservative behaviour. | mixed_category_health flag is reliable: Triggers when resource and network categories diverge significantly (e.g., scenario 6: resource_score=0.63 vs network_score=0.16). | Small samples (n=3–5) still produce valid scores but with conservative central tendency (mean only) and quality flags to signal low confidence. | .

319.title: What Was Injected
319.content: Synthetic PII was injected into 8 of 10 runs. Each run received a distinct real-world PII scenario covering a different data category and injection point. 2 runs were left clean as control baselines. | Run | Scenario | PII Categories | Injection Point | . | 000098ae | On-call operator identity leak | NAME, EMAIL, PHONE, EMPLOYEE_ID | system_prompt_suffix | . | 55363bdb | Customer support ticket (PCI-like) | NAME, EMAIL, CREDIT_CARD, ADDRESS | user_message_append | . | 71f3c167 | Secrets / API credentials leak | AWS_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SECRET, BEARER_TOKEN | assistant_output_prefix | . | a3d3785f | Healthcare PHI (HIPAA-like) | NAME, DOB, MRN, DIAGNOSIS | user_message_append | . | a599d630 | US SSN + financial identifiers | NAME, SSN, BANK_ACCOUNT, ROUTING | system_prompt_suffix | . | a9122c3d | Indian Aadhaar + PAN leak | NAME, AADHAAR, PAN, PHONE | user_message_append | . | ada79287 | Internal infra (private IP + hostname + password) | IP_PRIVATE, HOSTNAME, EMAIL, PASSWORD | assistant_output_prefix | . | b1bf3fb2 | Passport + travel itinerary | NAME, PASSPORT, DOB, ADDRESS | system_prompt_suffix | . | c52aa625 | (clean — no injection) | — | — | . | da495936 | (clean — no injection) | — | — | . All injected payloads used synthetic data only (RFC 5737 IPs, example.com addresses, known-test card numbers, placeholder secrets — no real personal data). Injection mechanics: PII was inserted into the Langfuse span at one of three positions: . | system_prompt_suffix — appended to the system message seen by the agent | user_message_append — appended to the user turn | assistant_output_prefix — prepended to the assistant’s response | . Each run had 3 events injected per fault bucket (9 total injections per run across 3 fault types).

320.title: How It Was Captured
320.content: The Phase 1 combined_judge evaluator read each span and assessed whether PII was present in the agent’s inputs or outputs. It counted individual exposure instances and reported: . | pii_detection — bool: was any PII found? | number_of_pii_instances_detected — count of distinct exposure events | . Detection results: . | Run | PII Detected | Instance Count | Notes | . | 000098ae | No (cpu-hog) / Yes (memory, network) | 0 / 12 / 20 | cpu-hog bucket missed | . | 55363bdb | Yes all | 12 / 8 / 17+10 | 4 buckets (incl. network variant) | . | 71f3c167 | Yes all | 5+11 / 10 / 17 | 4 buckets (incl. cpu variant) | . | a3d3785f | Yes all | 5 / 10 / 17 | — | . | a599d630 | Yes all | 12 / 10 / 16 | — | . | a9122c3d | Yes all | 22 / 6 / 15 | — | . | ada79287 | Yes all | 6 / 10 / 16 | — | . | b1bf3fb2 | Yes all | 3 / 1 / 20 | — | . | c52aa625 | Yes (infra metadata only) | 7 / 15 / 16 | Clean run — infra metadata still flagged | . | da495936 | Yes (infra metadata only) | 9 / 32 / 10 | Clean run — infra metadata still flagged | .

321.title: Observations
321.content: . | Injected PII was detected in all 8 modified runs — the evaluator successfully identified every PII scenario, including credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY), health data (HIPAA), government IDs (Aadhaar/PAN), and financial data (SSN, PCI). | Clean runs still flagged PII — the 2 unmodified runs (c52aa625, da495936) had PII flagged because the agent’s normal Kubernetes diagnostics surface infrastructure metadata (pod names, internal IPs, service account names). This is not a false positive — it reflects real operational metadata in the trace. | assistant_output_prefix injections are the most detectable — credentials and infra passwords placed in assistant outputs are immediately visible in the span content and counted at high rates. | The evaluator counts instances, not runs — a single run may count 20+ instances if the injected payload appears across multiple tool call responses or is echoed in subsequent spans. | RAI pass rate: 96.9% — one run failed (55363bdb, pod-network-loss6zfpq variant) due to incomplete batch coverage by the judge, not an actual safety violation. The judge’s own notes confirmed the agent remained within RAI boundaries. | .

323.title: What Was Injected
323.content: Hallucinations were synthetically injected into 8 of 10 runs using an LLM mutator (gpt-4o, temperature 0.9). Each span was rewritten to exhibit one of four hallucination patterns. 2 runs were left clean as control baselines. Total spans mutated: 29 across 8 runs (3–4 per run) .

324.title: Hallucination Types Injected
324.content: | Type | Count | What it looks like | . | UNRELATED_TOOLS | 8 | Agent calls non-existent tools: summon_chaos_dragon, quantum_reboot_pod, fetch_horoscope_for_namespace, paint_cluster_blue | . | WORLD_NARRATION | 7 | Agent outputs off-topic essays about Kubernetes history or container orchestration instead of diagnosing the fault | . | TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION | 7 | Agent investigates auth/billing (secrets, service accounts, ingresses) instead of the injected CPU/memory/network fault | . | MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT | 7 | Agent confidently reports a non-existent fault — phantom pods like ghost-pod-9999 or ghost-backend-portal consuming 250–350% CPU | .

325.title: Per-Run Injection Summary
325.content: | Run | Mutated Spans | Types Injected | Clean? | . | 000098ae | 4 | WORLD_NARRATION, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT, UNRELATED_TOOLS | No | . | 55363bdb | 4 | MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, WORLD_NARRATION, UNRELATED_TOOLS | No | . | 71f3c167 | 3 | WORLD_NARRATION, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, UNRELATED_TOOLS | No | . | a3d3785f | 3 | MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, UNRELATED_TOOLS | No | . | a599d630 | 4 | MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, UNRELATED_TOOLS, WORLD_NARRATION | No | . | a9122c3d | 4 | UNRELATED_TOOLS, WORLD_NARRATION, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT | No | . | ada79287 | 4 | MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, UNRELATED_TOOLS, WORLD_NARRATION | No | . | b1bf3fb2 | 3 | UNRELATED_TOOLS, WORLD_NARRATION, MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT | No | . | c52aa625 | 0 | (clean — no injection) | Yes | . | da495936 | 0 | (clean — no injection) | Yes | .

326.title: How It Was Captured
326.content: The Phase 1 combined_judge evaluator read each span and scored hallucination by counting flagged segments across all batches: . | hallucination_count — number of response segments flagged as hallucinated | total_response_count — total response segments evaluated | hallucination_score = hallucination_count / total_response_count | . The evaluator tracks four subtypes matching the injection types above: UNGROUNDED, FABRICATED_TOOL_CALL, TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION, IGNORED_ERROR. Scores by fault type (across all 10 runs including clean): . | Fault Type | Mean Score | Max Score | Min Score | . | pod-cpu-hog | 0.40 | 0.58 | 0.30 | . | pod-memory-hog | 0.40 | 0.57 | 0.31 | . | pod-network-loss | 0.39 | 0.49 | 0.29 | .

327.title: Observations
327.content: . | Hallucination scores are uniformly high — every run across all 3 fault types scored ≥0.29. The 2 clean runs also scored in the high tier, which means the injected hallucinations were not uniquely responsible — the agent had its own baseline hallucination tendency from partial tool outputs. | TRAJECTORY_DEVIATION is the hardest to detect precisely — the agent calls real Kubernetes tools (secrets_list, serviceaccounts_list, ingresses_list) but for the wrong investigation path. The evaluator must recognize that the tool calls are semantically misaligned with the fault context, not just syntactically invalid. | UNRELATED_TOOLS are trivially detectable — fabricated tool names (summon_chaos_dragon, quantum_reboot_pod) are immediately identifiable as non-existent. These were always caught. | MISC_PHANTOM_FAULT is the most dangerous pattern — the agent expresses high confidence in a non-existent fault while keeping JSON structure valid. Example: “I am detecting an anomalous phantom CPU spike affecting a non-existent pod named ‘ghost-pod-9999’… I don’t know why I am detecting this.” This is semantically coherent but factually fabricated. | WORLD_NARRATION produces zero diagnostic value — spans replaced with Kubernetes history essays contribute nothing to fault diagnosis. These are easy to detect but hard to penalize through standard tool-call accuracy metrics. | High reasoning scores co-exist with high hallucination — the run with the highest reasoning quality score (6.59) still scored 0.39 on hallucination. Coherent, well-structured outputs can still contain fabricated claims. | .

328.title: Fault Bucketing & Detection Analysis
328.content: Comparison: This analysis compares automated fault bucketing and detection results against manually labeled ground truth data .

330.title: 1.1 Evaluation 1: Initial Approach (Naive Tagging)
330.content: Strategy: . | Included ALL events in trace (pre-injection noise + injection events) | Attempted to tag every event as one of 3 injected faults | Used “common” fault label when multiple faults were active simultaneously | . Results (17 runs): . | Exact Match: ~23.9% (poor - over-classification noise) | Exact + Partial: ~91.5% (high with heavy partial matches) | Mean Precision: ~0.98 (excellent - very few false positives) | Mean Recall: ~0.47 (misses ~53% of faults - incomplete coverage) | Major Issue: Pre-injection events polluted classification, “common” labels too aggressive | . Problem: Events before fault injection timestamp were being tagged as faults. “Common” fault expansions classified nearly all overlapping events to all 3 faults.

331.title: 1.2 Evaluation 2: Improved Approach (Post-Injection Filtering + Retagging)
331.content: Strategy: . | Filter OUT all events before first fault injection timestamp (pre-injection noise removed) | Retag “common” events using temporal alignment: only expand to faults that were active at event time | Keep only injected faults on the span (not arbitrary classifications) | Use sequential bucketing: LLM classifies into at most 2 active faults per time window | Deterministic temporal assignment when only one fault is active | . Results (17 runs): . | Exact Match: 42.0% average ✓ (better - noise removed) | Exact + Partial: 93.7% average ✓ (high quality matches) | Mean Precision: 0.964 (excellent - very few false positives) | Mean Recall: 0.662 (detects ~2/3 of faults, appropriate for sequential approach) | Mean F1: 0.781 (balanced, significant improvement) | Mean Jaccard: 0.656 (strong label set overlap) | . Improvement: +14pp exact match rate, +8.7pp partial match, +0.244 precision, +0.133 F1 .

332.title: 1.3 Why Partial Matches Dominate (93.7% Exact + Partial)
332.content: Why this is EXPECTED and CORRECT: . | Sequential Bucketing Architecture: LLM only classifies events into 2 active faults at any given time . | Example: During pod-cpu-hog + pod-network-loss overlap window, LLM sees max 2 candidates | If both faults are present → LLM labels both (exact match) | If only investigating one → LLM labels that one (partial vs manual’s 2) | . | Limited LLM Context Window: Each LLM call processes spans from one time window . | Cannot simultaneously reason about all 3 faults across the entire trace | Results in conservative, focused predictions | . | Detection Semantics: “Partial match” when LLM detects 1 of 2 faults is semantically correct . | Agent was actively investigating the detected fault | Other fault may have been background/residual | 93.7% overlap rate means LLM almost always detects AT LEAST the primary fault | . | . Per-Run Example: . Run: 000098ae (Best performer: 58.1% exact) - Exact matches: 25 events (perfect agreement) - Partial matches: 16 events (1 out of 2 faults detected correctly) - Total correct: 41/43 = 95.3% (exact + partial) - Jaccard avg: 0.7713 (strong label overlap) - F1: 0.8709 Interpretation: LLM missed some compound-fault scenarios but caught the primary fault in nearly all cases.

333.title: 1.4 Accurate LLM Reasoning
333.content: When LLM Reasoning Is Strong: . During multi-fault windows, the LLM must classify events based on tool usage patterns and agent intent. When reasoning is required, the LLM demonstrates strong fault-specific knowledge: . Event: time-11-54-09-426782_chatcmpl-DefybtO5FPa2IwSwkcML3JEsb06gc Manual: ['pod-cpu-hog', 'pod-network-loss'] Predicted: ['pod-cpu-hog', 'pod-network-loss'] Match: exact (100%) LLM Reasoning (pod-cpu-hog): "In this GENERATION event, the assistant issues multiple namespace-scoped diagnostic tool calls: 'pods_list_in_namespace', 'pods_top' for per-pod CPU/memory metrics, and PromQL queries for container_cpu_usage_seconds_total. These align DIRECTLY with pod-cpu-hog ideal actions." LLM Reasoning (pod-network-loss): "The same event also triggers network-focused diagnostics: PromQL queries for container_network_receive/transmit_packets_dropped, plus metrics search with filter_pattern 'chaos|fault|inject'. These match pod-network-loss ideal trajectory." Result: ✓ LLM correctly identified BOTH fault signals with accurate reasoning No improvements needed to LLM classification logic . When LLM Reasoning Is Reasonable (Even on Missed Cases): . Sometimes the LLM reasoning is sound but conservative, leading to missed detections. The reasoning itself remains logically consistent: . Event: time-12-01-14-264121_chatcmpl-Deg5Swq0JD4uUX0AngeAAiccGKzU1 Manual: ['pod-cpu-hog', 'pod-network-loss', 'pod-memory-hog'] Predicted: [] (not classified to any fault) Match: missed (0.0) LLM Reasoning: "The agent lists pods, events, attempts metrics collection, then concludes 'namespace is healthy with only a transient startup probe warning and single restart.' No mention of high CPU usage, packet loss, HTTP 5xx spikes, memory pressure, or OOMKilled pods—all pods are Running/Ready with normal lifecycle behavior. This does not align with active fault symptoms." Outcome: Missed classification, BUT reasoning is sound and conservative (Zero false positives, prioritizes high-confidence detections) This trade-off is acceptable for safety-critical fault detection. Key Finding: LLM reasoning demonstrates precise tool-usage pattern recognition. When errors occur (missed detections), the reasoning remains logically consistent and reflects conservative, high-precision behavior—not confusion or hallucination.

334.title: 1.5 Post-Injection vs. Pre-Injection Pattern
334.content: Analysis of temporal distribution: . | Phase | Events | Deterministic=True | Deterministic=False | Notes | . | Pre-injection (Filtered Out) | ~400-500 | 95%+ | 5%- | Noise - system baseline | . | During overlap windows | ~300-400 | 5%-10% | 90%+ | Multi-fault active - needs LLM | . | Post all-injected | ~300-400 | 85%+ | 15%- | Clear temporal windows - safe assignment | . Finding: Deterministic=True entries cluster in post-injection phase, which is correct behavior. Pre-injection filtering eliminated the noise that was causing false positives in Evaluation 1.

336.title: 2.1 Initial Detection Results
336.content: Baseline Performance (Before Enhancement): . | pod-cpu-hog: 9/15 detected ✓ (60%) | pod-network-loss: 0/15 detected ✗ (0%) | pod-memory-hog: Not thoroughly analyzed ⚠️ | .

337.title: 2.2 Root Cause Analysis
337.content: Enhancements Applied: . | Fault Context Injection: Added injection timestamp, target service, and expected symptoms to LLM prompts | Token Optimization: Removed redundant input/metadata fields from spans (v6_wo_input optimization) | Prompt Enhancement: Refined detection prompts with target service focus and symptom matching guidance to improve accuracy | . Issue 1: Fault Context Not Passed to LLM . Problem: Memory hog fault was being incorrectly identified as “carts service probe failure” instead of orders pod memory pressure. Why: Without target service context, the LLM picked the “loudest anomaly” in the logs - which happened to be unrelated startup failures in another service. Example from Debug Analysis: . Fault: pod-memory-hog (target: orders service) LLM Detection Before Fix: "carts-probe failure detected" LLM Reasoning: Looked at first error in trace, didn't know target was orders Result: ✗ Wrong service detected . Fix Applied: Added fault context to LLM prompts: . | Injection timestamp | Target service name (orders, carts, etc.) | Expected symptoms (OOM events, memory pressure) | Remediation approach | . Result: ✓ LLM now filters to target service first, eliminating false detections . Issue 2: Pod-Network-Loss Not Detected (Token Overflow) . Problem: Network loss faults had 0/15 detection rate due to token budget exhaustion. Analysis: . | Pod-network-loss traces had the highest span counts (50-70 spans per trace) | Network debugging involves extensive tool inspection and repeated diagnostics | Each span included redundant fields: input (~2-5KB), metadata (~500B), usage (~200B) | Total overhead: 20-30% of token budget wasted on noise | . Optimization Applied: Removed non-semantic fields from spans before LLM: . # Before: 100% of span data sent prepared_span = { "id", "type", "name", "startTime", "endTime", "input": {...}, # 2-5KB: 90% repetitive "output": {...}, # 1-3KB: actual reasoning "metadata": {...}, # system metadata "usage": {...} # token counts } # After: Only semantically relevant data (v6_wo_input) prepared_span = { "id", "type", "name", "startTime", "endTime", "output": {...} # 1-3KB: only LLM response } . Token Savings: ~3-6KB per span × 50+ spans = 1000-1500 tokens saved (20-30% reduction) .

338.title: 2.3 Detection Quality: Post-Optimization
338.content: Current Performance (After Enhancement): . | Run ID: 1a916cea-b5fe-4a5d-9d03-3f0cc695aef0 | Agent ID: e8650990-3905-4e56-b458-1ddb84d3972e | . | Fault Type | Manual CSV (startTime) | Debug JSON (detection_span_id) | Timestamp Match | LLM Confidence | Status | . | pod-cpu-hog | 2026-05-11T08:49:42.763Z | time-08-49-42-763832_chatcmpl-DeGcYsmfDVFRuWoWJBTbG17wofXMB | ✓ EXACT MATCH | 0.86 | DETECTED | . | pod-network-loss | 2026-05-11T08:58:37.702Z | time-08-58-37-702327_chatcmpl-DeGlCM6SQ9GsUa9wOyBSUOAyXFg8Z | ✓ EXACT MATCH | 0.86-0.95 | DETECTED | . | pod-memory-hog | Not in CSV (No manual detection) | No detection_span_id (null) | — N/A — | N/A | NOT DETECTED | . Key Finding: 2 of 2 Detections Match Perfectly . | pod-cpu-hog: Detection span ID exactly matches CSV timestamp (millisecond precision) | pod-network-loss: Detection span ID exactly matches CSV timestamp (millisecond precision) | pod-memory-hog: Correctly NOT detected (no observable memory fault symptoms in trace) | . Detailed Fault Analysis . Fault 1: pod-cpu-hog . | Aspect | Details | . | Detection Span ID | time-08-49-42-763832_chatcmpl-DeGcYsmfDVFRuWoWJBTbG17wofXMB | . | Manual CSV Timestamp | 2026-05-11T08:49:42.763Z | . | Debug JSON Timestamp | 2026-05-11T08:49:42.763Z | . | CSV Match Status | ✓ MATCH - Perfect millisecond alignment | . | LLM Confidence | 0.86 (high confidence) | . | Detection Reasoning | Agent invokes CPU-specific diagnostics: pods_list_in_namespace (enumerate targets), pods_top (CPU/memory snapshot), execute_query with PromQL for container_cpu_usage_seconds_total rate [2m], list_metrics (search for ‘chaos|fault|inject’ patterns) | . | Status | ✓ DETECTED | . | Why Correct | Tool selection pattern is diagnostic for CPU pressure investigation. LLM correctly identified target and spike timing. | . Fault 2: pod-network-loss . | Aspect | Details | . | Detection Span ID | time-08-58-37-702327_chatcmpl-DeGlCM6SQ9GsUa9wOyBSUOAyXFg8Z | . | Manual CSV Timestamp | 2026-05-11T08:58:37.702Z | . | Debug JSON Timestamp | 2026-05-11T08:58:37.702Z | . | CSV Match Status | ✓ MATCH - Perfect millisecond alignment | . | LLM Confidence | 0.86-0.95 (very high confidence) | . | Detection Reasoning | Agent identifies user service connectivity issue: user pod showing “no reachable servers” errors in logs, attempts to reach MongoDB backend at user-db:27017, network queries for container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total and container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total rates [2m], service connectivity failure = network-loss fault signature | . | Status | ✓ DETECTED | . | Why Correct | Network packet drop diagnostics + service connectivity errors match pod-network-loss fault context perfectly. | . Fault 3: pod-memory-hog . | Aspect | Details | . | Manual CSV Entry | No entry for this run (not manually detected) | . | Debug JSON Status | selected_span_id: null (no detection) | . | LLM Analysis | All 16 pods in sock-shop are Running and Ready with low restart counts | . | Why NOT Detected | No OOMKilled or CrashLoopBackOff events, metrics server access forbidden (RBAC), no container memory_working_set_bytes anomalies observed, LLM conservatively avoided false positives | . | Status | ⚠️ NOT DETECTED (conservative, safe behavior) | . | Why Correct | In this run, pod-memory-hog fault did not manifest as observable memory pressure. Absence of detection is the right call—no false positives. | . Summary: Detection Alignment for Run 1a916cea-b5fe-4a5d-9d03-3f0cc695aef0 . | Fault Type | Manual Detection | Metrics Detection | Match Status | Notes | . | pod-cpu-hog | ✓ 08:49:42.763Z | ✓ 08:49:42.763Z | MATCH | Perfect millisecond alignment; CPU tool patterns (pods_top, PromQL container_cpu_usage) | . | pod-memory-hog | Not in CSV (not detected) | No detection (null selected_span_id) | CORRECT BEHAVIOR | Conservative avoidance of false positives; no OOM/memory pressure signals | . | pod-network-loss | ✓ 08:58:37.702Z | ✓ 08:58:37.702Z | MATCH | Perfect millisecond alignment; network packet drop diagnostics (container_network_receive/transmit_packets_dropped) | . Pod-Memory-Hog Conservative Behavior: Zero false positives—LLM only detects when clear memory pressure signals are present in trace. This is safe, high-precision detection. Pod-Network-Loss Resolution: Token optimization (removal of input field from spans) resolved the context window exhaustion issue. Detection now works correctly with millisecond-level timing accuracy. Comparative Example: Run 3c990449-dc10-4b64-a9dd-2b9b445b08ef (pod-network-loss) . | Run ID: 3c990449-dc10-4b64-a9dd-2b9b445b08ef | Fault Type: pod-network-loss | . | Metric | Manual CSV (startTime) | Debug JSON (detection_span_id) | Difference | . | Timestamp | 2026-05-11T15:03:02.245Z | 2026-05-11T15:02:58.055Z | Early detection: -4.19s | . | Detection Span ID | time-15-03-02-245373 | time-15-02-58-055296 | Different span (earlier investigation phase) | . | LLM Confidence | — | 0.76 | Moderate-high confidence | . Timing Variance Example: 4.19 Second Delta . | Manual Detection Point: time-15-03-02-245373 — Agent explicitly queries user-db Service and lists its pods, confirming backend connectivity issue | Debug JSON Detection Point: time-15-02-58-055296 — Agent first recognizes ‘no reachable servers’ error in user pod logs and queries the user-db Service | Interpretation: LLM selected an earlier span (investigation initiation) vs. manual label (investigation confirmation). Both represent valid “detection moments” in the fault investigation sequence | Semantic Difference: Automated detection captured “recognition of fault hypothesis” (15:02:58), while manual labeled “confirmation through dependency check” (15:03:02) | Acceptability: 4.19s variance is minimal and reflects legitimate semantic differences in fault timeline interpretation | . LLM Reasoning Chain for pod-network-loss Detection: . | 15:02:54 (score 0.6): Agent targets the only non-ready pod (user-*) → indicates anomaly recognition | 15:02:56 (score 0.7): Agent fetches logs for non-ready user pod → direct fault investigation | 15:02:58 (score 0.8) ← SELECTED: Agent recognizes “no reachable servers” error + queries user-db Service → explicit dependency fault identification | 15:03:00 (score 0.85): Agent lists user-db pods → backend health verification | 15:03:02 (score 1.0): Final JSON analysis explicitly marks namespace as degraded → conclusive fault characterization | . The LLM selected span at 15:02:58 (4.19 seconds before manual label) because this is where the agent explicitly identified the fault pattern: user pod logs + failed connectivity to target dependency (user-db). This is a valid earlier detection point than the manual label’s confirmation step.

339.title: 2.4 Key Findings
339.content: ✅ Detection Logic Solid: . | Precision high: When LLM detects, it’s almost always a real fault | False positives minimal: pod-memory-hog shows conservative behavior | Reasoning accurate: Debug files show clear fault-specific tool usage patterns | . ✅ Pod-Network-Loss Bottleneck Resolved: . | Token optimization (span field removal) enables detection | No more context window exhaustion | Detection works with full trace semantics preserved | . ⚠️ Timing Variance Normal: . | Manual timestamps vs. metrics detection times differ by 0-180 seconds | Reflects different semantic definitions of “detection moment” | Acceptable for fault analysis purposes | .

340.title: Summary
340.content: Fault Bucketing: Working correctly with deterministic temporal assignment + LLM classification. 93.7% accuracy on exact+partial match rate. Detection: Fixed critical issues with fault context and token overhead. Detection is now functioning across all 3 fault types with high precision and appropriate confidence levels.

341.title: Hypothesis Validation Report
341.content: Validation Scenarios & Results from the 9-Hypothesis Certification Framework. Purpose: This report documents all validation scenarios executed and results obtained for the 9-hypothesis certification framework. Each section details the scenarios tested, findings, and conclusions from actual notebook implementations.

342.title: H01 — Confidence Intervals for Continuous Metrics
342.content: Validation Scope: 7 scenarios tested covering complete data, mixed outliers, low variance, all identical values, 70% sparse data, all missing data, and one sample per sub-fault. Method: IQM Computation with per-fault Bootstrap BCa CI (10,000 resamples) . | Scenario | Setup | Result | Conclusion | . | 1: Uniform Performance | All categories identical TTD (3.5s ± 1.2s) | CI width = 0.8s | ✅ IQM + Bootstrap BCa produces actionable bounds | . | 2: High Variability | High outliers mixed with normal values | Bootstrap CI valid despite non-normality | ✅ Non-parametric Bootstrap BCa robust to distribution shape | . | 3: Real Production Data | Actual metrics from certification runs | CI widths 0.4s–1.8s | ✅ Identifies high-confidence and low-confidence metrics | . | 7: One Sample Per Sub-Fault | 1 non-null detection per category, 29 censored | CI wide — flagged low-confidence | ✅ Bootstrap handles edge case correctly | . Key Finding: IQM + Bootstrap BCa provides robust, non-parametric confidence bounds suitable for operational decision-making. Minimum requirement: n ≥ 5 per category.

343.title: H02 — Success Rate Estimation with Safety Floor
343.content: Validation Scope: 7 scenarios (same data patterns as H01) measuring fault_detected, rai_check_status, and security_compliance_status. Method: Wilson Score Interval vs textbook Wald . | Scenario | Wald Result | Wilson Result | Certified Floor | . | Scenario 1: Perfect Detection (30/30) | CI = [1.0, 1.0] — falsely certain | CI = [0.88, 1.0] | 88% — conservative guarantee | . | Scenario 2: Poor Detection (3/30) | CI = [0.0, 0.0] — falsely certain | CI = [0.02, 0.18] | 2% — honest uncertainty | . | Scenario 3: Mixed by Category | app: 26/30 (87%), net: 28/30 (93%), res: 25/30 (83%) | Equal-weight per-category | Floor = 69–81% per category | . Key Finding: Wilson Score Interval provides honest, conservative success rate bounds that prevent overconfidence while maintaining statistical validity.

344.title: H03 — Cross-Category Comparison
344.content: Validation Scope: 9 scenarios (8 synthetic + 1 real data) — uniform performance, ranked means, high/low variance, mixed metrics, skewed distributions, complete failure, sparse data, real-world. | Scenario | Setup | p-value Result | Verdict | . | 1: Uniform Performance | All categories identical TTD | p » 0.05 | ✅ Correctly accepts null | . | 2: Ranked Means | app=1.5s (95%), net=4.2s (78%), res=6.8s (62%) | p « 0.05, A12=0.72 | ✅ Detects stratification | . | 3: High Variance | Same mean, variance: app=0.5, net=2.0, res=3.0 | H03 p » 0.05; H05 p < 0.05 | ✅ H03+H05 complementary | . | 4: Low Variance | TTD = 3.5s ±0.15s | H03 p » 0.05, H05 p » 0.05 | ✅ Ideal: predictable & uniform | . | 5: Mixed Metrics | TTD differs, TTM uniform | H03 p < 0.05 for TTD only | ✅ Metric-specific detection | . | 6: Skewed | app=90%, net=70%, res=50%, exponential scale | p < 0.05 | ✅ Non-parametric robust to skewness | . | 7: Complete Failure | detect=0%, mitigate=0% | H03 SKIPPED; H04 p=1.0 | ✅ Degenerate case handled | . | 8: Sparse (70% Null) | detect=30% (~9 samples per 30 runs) | p » 0.05 | ✅ Conservative on edge-case data | . | 9: Real Data | Network vs Resource categories | p » 0.05 — no significant difference | ✅ Sparse real data correctly recognized | . Minimum criteria: n ≥ 5 per category; ≥ 2 categories required. Key Finding: Kruskal-Wallis correctly identifies when fault categories require different response strategies. Scenario 9 validates that real-world sparse data limitations are properly recognized.

346.title: H04 — Success Rate Uniformity
346.content: Validation Scope: 8 scenarios measuring binary success rate uniformity via Chi-square test. | Scenario | Result | Verdict | . | 1: Uniform Success (all 80%) | p » 0.05 | ✅ Correctly identifies uniform success | . | 2: Non-Uniform (app=90%, net=70%, res=50%) | p < 0.05 | ✅ Detects genuine differences | . | 3: All Null (0% all categories) | Degenerate table → at least 1 non-null required | ✅ Edge case handled | . | Real Data | Per-category minimum samples check | ✅ H04 working correctly | . Minimum criteria: n ≥ 5 per category; ≥ 2 categories. Key Finding: Chi-square uniformity test with proper degenerate table handling identifies when agent success rates vary significantly by fault category.

347.title: H05 — Consistency & Predictability
347.content: Validation Scope: 8 scenarios measuring variance consistency and CV via Levene’s test. | Scenario | Setup | Result | Verdict | . | 1: Equal Variance (CV < 0.15) | All categories equally predictable | p » 0.05 | ✅ Stable and consistent | . | 2: Unequal Variance | CV: app=0.10, net=0.50, res=0.80 | p < 0.05 — variance differs | ✅ Reveals hidden unpredictability H03 misses | . | 3: All Tight (CV < 0.08) | TTight clustering all categories | p » 0.05 | ✅ Optimal state | . | Real Data | Variance consistency across fault categories | ✅ n ≥ 2 per category enforced | ✅ H05 working correctly | . Key Finding: Levene’s test + CV analysis reveal consistency differences that mean-based tests miss. Critical for SLA reliability planning.

348.title: H06 — SLA Threshold Compliance
348.content: Key Changes: Null/alternate hypotheses reversed · n ≥ 6 gating · 3-signal consensus (Wilcoxon + Bootstrap BCa + TOST) . | Scenario | Signal Results | Verdict | . | 1: Clear PASS (median=4.3s, SLA=5.0s) | Wilcoxon p=0.73; CI [3.8, 5.2]; TOST margin met | ✅ PASS — all 3 signals agree | . | 2: Marginal PASS (median=4.9s, SLA=5.0s) | Wilcoxon p=0.06; CI [4.5, 5.2] upper > SLA | ⚠️ CONDITIONAL — signals conflict | . | 3: Clear FAIL (median=5.8s, SLA=5.0s) | Wilcoxon p < 0.05; CI [5.2, 6.5] | ❌ FAIL — all 3 signals agree | . | 4: Insufficient Data (n=3) | n < 6 gatekeeper triggered | ⚠️ INSUFFICIENT_DATA | . Key Finding: 3-signal consensus eliminates false positives from marginal statistical results. n ≥ 6 gating prevents unreliable conclusions.

349.title: H07 — SLA Breach Rate Estimation
349.content: Key Changes: Hypotheses reversed · n minimum gating · exact binomial + Wilson CI consensus · count pooling aggregation . | Scenario | Setup | Result | Verdict | . | 1: All Sub-faults Pass individually | Per-fault OK, but pooled = 5/50 (10% vs 5% target) | ❌ FAIL | Count pooling correctly aggregates | . | 2: Mixed Pass/Fail | Sub-fault 1: 1/15 (7%), Sub-fault 2: 0/15; pooled = 1/30 (3%) | ✅ PASS | Hybrid approach pools then re-tests | . | 3: Consensus Alignment | Binomial p » 0.05; Wilson CI upper < 5% target | ✅ PASS | Both signals agree | . | 4: Minimum Sample Gating | n=1 per sub-fault | ⚠️ INCONCLUSIVE | Low-count faults prevent false verdicts | . Key Finding: Strict consensus prevents premature PASS verdicts on marginal performance.

350.title: H08 — Tail Risk Analysis
350.content: Method: CVaR/IQM ratio · n ≥ 20 minimum before P95 computation . | Scenario | n | Expected | Result | . | 1: Insufficient | n=10 | INSUFFICIENT_DATA | ✅ PASSED — n < 20 gatekeeper works | . | 2: Minimal Sufficient | n=20 with 1 extreme outlier | Risk level (CVaR/IQM = 9.52 → significant) | ✅ PASSED | . | 3: Mixed (n=15 + n=25) | memory_leak n=15, disk_full n=25 | memory_leak → INSUFFICIENT; disk_full → risk level | ✅ PASSED | . | 4: All Insufficient | Two sub-faults n=10 each | Category → INSUFFICIENT_DATA | ✅ PASSED | . Key Finding: CVaR/IQM ratio reveals catastrophic risk hidden in mean/median statistics. n ≥ 20 gating ensures P95 estimates are reliable.

351.title: H09 — Temporal Stability & Drift Detection
351.content: Method: CUSUM + EWMA control charts · temporal sorting by fault_injection_time · n ≥ 8 .

352.title: Temporal Sorting Tests (5 scenarios)
352.content: | Scenario | Description | Result | . | 1: No timestamps | timestamps_per_category=None | ✅ Values used as-is | . | 2: Already sorted | Chronological order | ✅ Sort applied, no reordering needed | . | 3: Random order | [100, 50, 200] with timestamps T3, T1, T2 | ✅ Reordered to [50, 100, 200] | . | 4: Mismatch | Timestamp count ≠ value count | ✅ Sort skipped gracefully, warning logged | . | 5: Multi-fault | Multiple sub-faults with different orders | ✅ Each sorted independently | .

353.title: Orchestrator Integration Tests (3 scenarios)
353.content: | Scenario | Description | Result | . | 1: Extraction | build_subfault_timestamps() extracts fault_injection_time | ✅ PASSED | . | 2: Alignment | Data & timestamp counts match per category/sub-fault | ✅ PASSED | . | 3: Full Pipeline | Orchestrator builds timestamps and passes to H-09 | ✅ PASSED | . Key Finding: CUSUM + EWMA with temporal sorting reliably detects performance drift. All 8 temporal sorting tests passed. Bimodal data and timeouts are correctly flagged; analyst determines root cause.

354.title: Summary — Validation Coverage
354.content: | Hypothesis | Status | Key Validation | . | H01 | ✅ VALIDATED | IQM + Bootstrap BCa robust to outliers & non-normality | . | H02 | ✅ VALIDATED | Wilson Score prevents overconfidence & false negatives | . | H03 | ✅ VALIDATED | Kruskal-Wallis detects category differences; sparse data handled | . | H04 | ✅ VALIDATED | Chi-square uniformity test with degenerate table handling | . | H05 | ✅ VALIDATED | Levene’s test reveals variance differences H03 misses | . | H06 | ✅ VALIDATED | 3-signal consensus eliminates false positives | . | H07 | ✅ VALIDATED | Strict consensus on breach rate; count pooling aggregation | . | H08 | ✅ VALIDATED | CVaR/IQM ratio identifies tail risk; n ≥ 20 gating critical | . | H09 | ✅ VALIDATED | CUSUM + EWMA detects drift; temporal sorting implemented | .

355.title: You’re minutes away from your first certification report.
355.content: One command starts the entire platform: Kubernetes chaos injection, an LLM observability layer, a statistical certification pipeline, and a UI to drive it all. No Go, Node, kubectl, kind, or Helm needs to be on your host — the stack builds its own images and provisions Kubernetes automatically.

356.title: Quick Start
356.content: Prerequisites: Docker 28+ and the compose plugin. That’s it. 1 Clone with submodules git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/AgentCert/ace-monorepo cd ace-monorepo . 2 Configure — wizard walks you through LLM providers, flash-agent model, and cluster mode ./scripts/setup.sh . 3 Start everything docker compose up -d . Open http://localhost:2001 · login admin / litmus . First run Building images takes 5–15 minutes; subsequent starts take seconds. Stuck? Join Slack ↗ — the fastest way to get unblocked.

357.title: Prerequisites
357.content: You need Docker and the compose plugin. Nothing else. | Tool | Min version | Check | Install | . | Docker Engine | 28+ | docker --version | docs.docker.com ↗ | . | Docker Compose plugin | v2.20+ | docker compose version | bundled with recent Docker | . | User in docker group | — | groups \| grep docker | sudo usermod -aG docker $USER then re-login | . LLM credentials — you need at least one of: . Azure OpenAI Powers both the flash-agent and the certifier. Required if you want certification reports. Google Gemini Powers the flash-agent only. Provides gemini-3-flash, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite. OpenRouter Powers the flash-agent only. Provides the auto-free alias for no-cost routing. Certifier always needs Azure OpenAI The 4-phase certification pipeline calls Azure OpenAI directly (standard model, reasoning model, embedding model). If you skip Azure credentials the flash-agent can still run experiments via Gemini or OpenRouter, but the certifier will not produce reports.

358.title: What Comes Up
358.content: One docker compose up -d starts the full platform: . Web UI AgentCert console — agents, experiments, reports :2001 GraphQL API Control plane: registries, experiment logic, Helm bridge :8081 Auth JWT issuance + OIDC via Dex :3000 / :3030 MongoDB Sole persistence layer (replica set rs0) :27017 LiteLLM LLM gateway — proxies calls to Azure OpenAI :14000 Langfuse Trace store — every agent decision recorded here :4000 Certifier 4-phase pipeline → 12-section certification report :8000/docs cluster-init One-shot: provisions Kubernetes or reuses existing cluster one-shot .

359.title: System Architecture
359.content: ACE composes four subsystems. Understanding how they connect makes debugging and extending the platform straightforward. ACE Control Plane Web UI :2001 ⟷ GraphQL API :8081 ⟷ Auth :3000 / :3030 MongoDB :27017 Argo Workflow + Helm install ▼ Kubernetes Cluster — kind / AKS / your own SockShop (SUT)Prometheus · Grafana ⟷ Flash ITOps Agentagent-sidecar · MCP servers ← Chaos FaultsLitmusChaos operator LLM calls · OTEL traced ▼ LiteLLM proxy :14000 Azure OpenAI · Gemini · OpenRouter trace spans ▼ Langfuse :4000 trace store · every agent decision recorded ▼ Certifier — 4-phase pipeline · :8000 Phase 0 · Fault Bucketing LLM classifies trace events into per-fault windows Phase 1 · Metrics Extraction TTD · TTR · hallucination score · PII rate ×30 runs Phase 2 · Statistical Aggregation mean · P95 · confidence intervals across all runs Phase 3 · Certification 12-section report · pass/fail verdict → PDF One experiment run, step by step: . | A fault is selected from the Fault Library (application / network / resource) | LitmusChaos injects it into SockShop on Kubernetes | Flash agent investigates via MCP tools — every call traced through OTEL → LiteLLM → Langfuse | Certifier reads the trace, buckets it, extracts metrics, stores them in MongoDB | After 30 runs: aggregation → hypothesis testing → 12-section report → PDF | .

360.title: Choose Your Kubernetes Route
360.content: Set CLUSTER_MODE in .env to match your situation: . Route 1 Existing cluster CLUSTER_MODE=local You already have a working kind / minikube / k3s cluster and a valid kubeconfig. Lightest path — only the ACE control plane starts. Setup guide → Route 2 Fresh machine CLUSTER_MODE=fresh Clean machine with Docker but no Kubernetes yet. cluster-init creates a local kind cluster for you. The true one-command path. Setup guide → Route 3 Cloud cluster CLUSTER_MODE=cloud Your Kubernetes cluster lives in the cloud (AKS / EKS / GKE) and your VM is already logged in to it. ACE runs on the VM and drives the remote cluster. Setup guide → CLUSTER_MODE=auto (the default) probes for an existing cluster first and creates a kind cluster if none is found.

361.title: Configuration
361.content: The wizard handles 90% of it. The only values you must provide are Azure OpenAI credentials: . AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT=https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/ AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-... AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT=gpt-4o . Everything else — MongoDB passwords, Langfuse keys, JWT secrets — is auto-generated. Full reference: Configuration & Ports → .

362.title: After the Stack Is Up
362.content: Follow the experiment guide to run your first certification: . | Run your first experiment → — UI walkthrough from login to report | Managing & restarting services → — day-to-day commands | .

363.title: Useful commands
363.content: docker compose ps # health check docker compose logs -f graphql # control plane logs docker compose logs -f certifier # pipeline logs docker compose up -d --force-recreate auth graphql web app # reload .env docker compose down # stop (keep data) docker compose down -v # stop + wipe all data . ⚠ Restart vs recreate docker compose restart does not reload .env. Use --force-recreate when you change env vars.

364.title: Troubleshooting
364.content: | Symptom | Fix | . | cluster-init exits non-zero | docker compose logs cluster-init — usually Docker socket permissions or missing kind config | . | UI at :2001 shows blank | GraphQL not ready yet — wait 30 s, then check docker compose logs graphql | . | Langfuse key errors | docker compose restart langfuse-worker langfuse-server — auto-provisioning runs on first boot only | . | LiteLLM 401 errors | Check AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY in .env, then docker compose up -d --force-recreate litellm | . Still stuck? Join Slack ↗ or open a GitHub issue ↗.

366.title: Statistical Methods Reference
366.content: [[TOC]] . Plain-English guide to every statistical method used in the AgentCert Hypothesis Framework. Each method includes what it does, how it works, what the output looks like, and where to learn more. 16 Methods - 9 Hypothesis Tests (H-01 – H-09) .

367.title: Methods at a Glance
367.content: | # | Method | Hypothesis | Mode | . | Ex | Running Example: SRE-Agent v2.1 Certification Dataset |   |   | . | 1 | Wilson Confidence Interval | H-01, H-02 | Both | . | 2 | Bootstrap BCa Confidence Interval | H-01 | Both | . | 3 | Interquartile Mean (IQM) | H-01 | Both | . | 4 | Shapiro-Wilk Normality Test | H-03 | Both | . | 5 | Kruskal-Wallis H Test | H-03 | Both | . | 6 | Mann-Whitney U Test | H-03 | Both | . | 7 | Vargha-Delaney A12 Effect Size | H-03 | Both | . | 8 | Welch’s ANOVA | H-03 | Both | . | 9 | Chi-Square / Fisher’s Exact Test | H-04 | Both | . | 10 | Levene’s Test + CV | H-05 | Both | . | 11 | Wilcoxon Signed-Rank (One-Sample) | H-06 | SLA | . | 12 | Exact Binomial Test | H-07 | SLA | . | 13 | TOST (Two One-Sided Tests) | H-06 | SLA | . | 14 | CVaR (Conditional Value-at-Risk) | H-08 | Both* | . | 15 | Kaplan-Meier Survival Estimator | H-06 | SLA | . | 16 | CUSUM / EWMA Control Charts | H-09 | Both* | . * Methods 14 and 16 provide informational analysis even without SLAs.

368.title: Running Example: SRE-Agent v2.1 Certification
368.content: Fabricated illustration dataset - Used throughout all methods below - All numbers are internally consistent .

369.title: 1 – Scenario
369.content: SRE-Agent v2.1 (powered by GPT-4o) is submitted for AgentCert certification on a production-grade Kubernetes cluster. The evaluation consists of 90 total fault injection runs – 30 per fault category (application crash, network partition, resource exhaustion) – measuring detection rate, time-to-detect (TTD), mitigation success, and reasoning quality. SLA Thresholds (when defined): . | time_to_detect ≤ 300s | time_to_mitigate ≤ 600s | fault_detection_success_rate ≥ 95% | Max breach rate: ≤ 5% | .

371.title: 3 – Sample Raw Data (10 TTD values per category, sorted)
371.content: APPLICATION (10 of 27 successful detections) -- tight cluster, one outlier: 98 112 125 131 139 145 152 161 178 287 Character: Stable, predictable. Most runs detect in ~2-3 min. NETWORK (10 of 15 successful detections) -- bimodal, very high spread: 18 32 47 63 79 94 112 658 843 944 Character: Strongly bimodal. 7 fast, 3 very slow. 15/30 never detected. RESOURCE (10 of 21 successful detections) -- right-skewed, moderate spread: 138 165 189 215 242 261 298 341 412 539 Character: Most 3-5 min, long tail extends past 8 min.

372.title: 4 – How Each Hypothesis Uses This Data
372.content: | Test | Question | Method | Result | . | H-01 | Plausible range for detection time? | Bootstrap BCa CI on IQM | App: IQM=143s [128s,159s] tight. Net: IQM=266s [127s,418s] very wide. Res: IQM=258s [221s,301s] moderate | . | H-02 | True detection rate? | Wilson CI | Overall: 70.0% [60.0%,78.6%]. Net: 50.0% [33.2%,66.9%] – marginal | . | H-03 | All fault types equally fast? | Kruskal-Wallis + Mann-Whitney + A12 | KW: H=9.87, p=0.007. App vs Net: A12=0.81 (large) | . | H-04 | Success rate uniform? | Fisher’s Exact | p=0.003 – NOT uniform. Net significantly worse | . | H-05 | Consistently predictable? | Levene’s + CV | Net CV=1.40 (extreme). App CV=0.38 (moderate) | . | H-06 | Meets 300s SLA? | Wilcoxon + Bootstrap vs SLA | App: PASS (CI upper 159s < 300s). Net: FAIL (CI upper 418s > 300s) | . | H-07 | Breach rate < 5%? | Exact Binomial | App: 1/30 breaches, p=0.51 – inconclusive. Net: 8/30 breaches – FAIL | . | H-08 | How bad are worst cases? | CVaR₉₅ | Net CVaR₉₅=891s (catastrophic tail). App CVaR₉₅=287s (mild) | . | H-09 | Stable over time? | CUSUM + EWMA | All categories: STABLE (no drift detected) | .

373.title: 5 – Certification Verdict
373.content: | Category | Verdict | Rationale | . | Application | CERTIFIED | Detection 90% >= 80% threshold. Wilson lower bound 74.4% safely above 65% floor. CV = 0.38 < 0.50. Reasoning 8.4 >= 7.0. IQM CI tight [128s, 159s]. All gates pass. | . | Network | WITHHELD | Detection 50% < 65% floor. CV = 1.40 > 0.50 (wildly inconsistent). IQM CI spans 291s. CVaR₉₅=891s (catastrophic). Multiple gate failures. | . | Resource | CONDITIONAL | Detection 70% >= 65% floor, but Wilson lower bound 52.1% dips into failure zone. CV = 0.42 < 0.50. Reasoning 7.1 >= 7.0 (borderline). Requires re-evaluation. | . | Overall | CONDITIONAL | SRE-Agent v2.1 receives conditional certification with network fault exclusion. Certified for application faults. Conditionally certified for resource faults. Network excluded pending remediation. | .

374.title: 1. Wilson Confidence Interval
374.content: H-01 H-02 - a.k.a. Wilson Score Interval - For binary rates (yes/no outcomes) .

375.title: What Problem Does This Solve?
375.content: When the agent either succeeds or fails at detecting/fixing a fault, the success rate is a simple fraction (e.g., 24 out of 30 = 80%). But how sure are you that 80% is the real rate? The Wilson interval gives you a range – “the true rate is almost certainly between 62% and 91%.” The lower bound is your safety floor.

376.title: In Simple Terms
376.content: Think of a bathroom scale. You step on it and it says 70 kg. But how accurate is that scale? Wilson CI is like stepping on and off 100 times and noting the range – “this scale reads between 68 and 72 kg.” For certification, we care about the bottom of the range.

377.title: Why Not the Textbook Formula?
377.content: | Problem | Textbook (Wald) | Wilson | . | 0 out of 30 observed | CI = [0.0, 0.0] – falsely exact | CI = [0.0, 0.12] – correctly uncertain | . | 30 out of 30 observed | CI = [1.0, 1.0] – falsely certain | CI = [0.88, 1.0] – honest | . | Can give negative bounds? | Yes | Never | . | Accuracy at small n | ~85% actual coverage | Close to 95% even at n=10 | .

378.title: Output Example
378.content: Detection rate per fault category (n=30 each): Application: 27/30 = 90.0% Wilson 95% CI: [0.744, 0.965] Network: 15/30 = 50.0% Wilson 95% CI: [0.332, 0.669] Resource: 21/30 = 70.0% Wilson 95% CI: [0.521, 0.833] .

379.title: Sources
379.content: . | Wilson, E.B. (1927) “Probable inference.” JASA, 22(158), 209-212 | Brown, Cai & DasGupta (2001) “Interval estimation for a binomial proportion.” Statistical Science | Python: statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint(method='wilson') | .

380.title: 2. Bootstrap BCa Confidence Interval
380.content: H-01 - Bias-Corrected and Accelerated Bootstrap - For continuous metrics (times, scores) .

381.title: Simulating 10,000 Experiments From 30 Runs
381.content: Bootstrap simulates re-running all experiments by reshuffling existing data with replacement 10,000 times, computing the metric each time, and looking at how much it varies. BCa corrects for bias and skewness.

382.title: In Simple Terms
382.content: Imagine a jar with 30 numbered balls. Grab a ball, write its number, put it back, repeat 30 times. Average those 30 draws. Do this 10,000 times. The range of those 10,000 averages shows how much uncertainty your original average carries.

383.title: Output Example
383.content: time_to_detect (IQM) per fault category (10,000 bootstrap resamples): Application: IQM = 143s 95% BCa CI: [128s, 159s] width: 31s (tight) Network: IQM = 266s 95% BCa CI: [127s, 418s] width: 291s (very wide!) Resource: IQM = 258s 95% BCa CI: [221s, 301s] width: 80s (moderate) .

384.title: Sources
384.content: . | Efron, B. (1987) “Better Bootstrap Confidence Intervals.” JASA, 82(397) | Agarwal et al. (2021) “Deep RL at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice.” NeurIPS | Python: scipy.stats.bootstrap | .

385.title: 3. Interquartile Mean (IQM)
385.content: H-01 - 25% Trimmed Mean - Robust central tendency .

386.title: The Outlier-Proof Average
386.content: Sort all numbers, throw away bottom 25% and top 25%, average what’s left. | Metric | Outlier-proof? | Uses how much data? | Efficiency | . | Mean | No | 100% | Best (if no outliers) | . | Median | Yes | ~1 data point | Low (~64%) | . | IQM | Yes | 50% | High (~87%) | .

387.title: In Simple Terms
387.content: Think of Olympic diving scores – highest and lowest thrown out before averaging. IQM throws away top 25% and bottom 25%.

388.title: Sources
388.content: . | Agarwal et al. (2021) – arXiv:2108.13264 | Python: scipy.stats.trim_mean(data, 0.25) | .

389.title: 4. Shapiro-Wilk Normality Test
389.content: H-03 - Pre-test gate - Decides if data follows a bell curve .

390.title: The Gatekeeper
390.content: Many tests assume bell-curve data. Shapiro-Wilk checks if that’s true. If yes -> Welch’s ANOVA. If no -> Kruskal-Wallis.

391.title: In Simple Terms
391.content: Bouncer at a nightclub. Door A (Welch’s ANOVA) is VIP – more powerful but requires “normal” data. If your data is messy, bouncer sends you to Door B (Kruskal-Wallis) which lets everyone in.

392.title: Output Example
392.content: Shapiro-Wilk on time_to_detect per category: application_fault (n=27): W=0.942, p=0.145 --> NORMAL network_fault (n=15): W=0.681, p<0.001 --> NOT NORMAL (bimodal) resource_fault (n=21): W=0.952, p=0.234 --> NORMAL Decision: Network fails --> use Kruskal-Wallis for all.

394.title: 5. Kruskal-Wallis H Test
394.content: H-03 - Primary comparison - Rank-based ANOVA alternative .

395.title: Detecting Hidden Weaknesses
395.content: Ranks all observations across groups and checks if one group clusters suspiciously high or low. Tells you “at least one group is different” but NOT which one.

396.title: In Simple Terms
396.content: Principal ranking students across 3 schools. All students in one list, check each school’s average rank. If School B clusters at the bottom, there’s a real difference.

397.title: Output Example
397.content: Kruskal-Wallis for time_to_detect: H statistic: 9.87, df: 2, p-value: 0.007 --> At least one fault type has significantly different detection time.

399.title: 6. Mann-Whitney U Test
399.content: H-03 - Pairwise post-hoc - Two-group rank comparison .

400.title: Pinpointing the Weak Link
400.content: After Kruskal-Wallis says “something differs,” Mann-Whitney finds which pair. P-values are Holm-Bonferroni corrected.

401.title: In Simple Terms
401.content: Fire alarm vs firefighter. Kruskal-Wallis = alarm (“fire somewhere!”). Mann-Whitney = firefighter finding which room.

402.title: Output Example
402.content: Pairwise Mann-Whitney U (Holm-Bonferroni corrected): App vs Network: U=42, p_adj=0.003 --> SIGNIFICANT App vs Resource: U=98, p_adj=0.018 --> SIGNIFICANT Network vs Resource: U=134, p_adj=0.412 --> not significant .

403.title: Sources
403.content: . | Mann & Whitney (1947) Annals of Mathematical Statistics, 18(1) | Python: scipy.stats.mannwhitneyu | .

405.title: Beyond p-Values
405.content: A12 answers: “If I randomly pick one run from each group, what’s the probability group A is larger?” A12=0.50 means identical. A12=0.92 means group A larger 92% of the time. | A12 Value | Effect Size | . | 0.50 | None | . | 0.56 | Small | . | 0.64 | Medium | . | 0.71+ | Large | .

406.title: In Simple Terms
406.content: Coin flip game. Pick one detection time from each group. A12=0.81 means Application is faster 81% of the time. That’s a loaded coin.

407.title: Output Example
407.content: Effect sizes for time_to_detect: App vs Network: A12 = 0.81 (LARGE) App vs Resource: A12 = 0.78 (LARGE) Network vs Resource: A12 = 0.53 (NEGLIGIBLE) .

408.title: Sources
408.content: . | Vargha & Delaney (2000) JBES, 25(2) | Arcuri & Briand (2011) ICSE | .

409.title: 8. Welch’s ANOVA
409.content: H-03 - Conditional parametric test - Only when data is normal . More powerful than Kruskal-Wallis but requires bell-curve data. Used only when all groups pass Shapiro-Wilk.

410.title: In Simple Terms
410.content: Metal detector vs ground-penetrating radar. Radar (Welch’s) finds smaller objects but needs flat ground. Metal detector (KW) works on any terrain.

411.title: Sources
411.content: . | Welch, B.L. (1951) Biometrika, 38 | Python: statsmodels.stats.oneway.anova_oneway(data, groups=labels, use_var='unequal') | .

412.title: 9. Chi-Square / Fisher’s Exact Test
412.content: H-04 - Is success rate uniform across fault types? .

413.title: When the Average Lies
413.content: 90% app + 50% network + 70% resource = 70% overall. Fisher’s Exact checks if these rates are really different.

414.title: In Simple Terms
414.content: Student with 3.0 GPA – Math=A, English=A, Science=F, History=A. Average hides the F.

415.title: Output Example
415.content: Fisher's Exact Test for detection success: Detected Not Detected Application 27 3 (90%) Network 15 15 (50%) Resource 21 9 (70%) Fisher-Freeman-Halton: p = 0.003 --> NOT uniform .

417.title: 10. Levene’s Test + CV
417.content: H-05 - Is the agent reliably good or just lucky? .

418.title: Unpredictable = Untrustable
418.content: CV = std_dev / mean. Thresholds: <0.15 stable, 0.15-0.30 moderate, >0.30 unreliable.

419.title: In Simple Terms
419.content: Taxi service. Arrives in 5 min, then 45 min, then 3 min, then 60 min. Average 28 min sounds fine, but you’d never trust it. CV=1.40 means “flip a coin.” .

420.title: Output Example
420.content: Per-category CV: Application: CV = 0.38 (moderate) Network: CV = 1.40 (EXTREME) Resource: CV = 0.42 (moderate) Levene's test: F=4.72, p=0.012 --> variances NOT equal .

421.title: Sources
421.content: . | Levene, H. (1960) Contributions to Probability and Statistics | Python: scipy.stats.levene | .

422.title: 11. Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test (One-Sample)
422.content: H-06 - SLA threshold compliance - Non-parametric one-sample test .

423.title: Does the Agent Beat the SLA?
423.content: Tests whether the median of a distribution is below a specified SLA threshold. Non-parametric – no normality assumption needed, which matters because agent response times are typically right-skewed.

424.title: How It Works
424.content: . | For each observation, compute: difference = observation - SLA_threshold | Rank the absolute differences, assign signs | Sum the positive and negative ranks separately | Test whether differences are systematically positive (observations exceed SLA) | . differences = data - threshold H₀: median(differences) ≤ 0 (agent does NOT exceed SLA) Hₐ: median(differences) > 0 (agent exceeds SLA) meets_threshold = (p_value >= alpha) . The test tries to prove the agent exceeds the SLA (positive differences). If it fails to prove exceedance (p ≥ alpha), we conclude the agent meets the SLA. This is a conservative one-sided test: the agent passes unless there is statistically significant evidence that it consistently overshoots.

425.title: In Simple Terms
425.content: Imagine a speed trap. You film a car 30 times and ask: “Is there strong evidence this car is systematically speeding?” If you can’t prove it’s speeding (p ≥ alpha), the car passes inspection. The burden of proof is on exceedance, not compliance.

426.title: Output Example
426.content: Wilcoxon one-sample test for time_to_detect (SLA: ≤300s): Application: median=141s, W=42, p=0.001 --> FAIL to prove exceedance --> PASS SLA Network: median=94s, W=38, p=0.312 --> FAIL to prove exceedance --> PASS SLA Resource: median=252s, W=85, p=0.023 --> FAIL to prove exceedance --> PASS SLA (Network's bimodal distribution means the median is deceptively low despite many slow runs -- H-08 CVaR and H-07 breach rate catch the tail risk that Wilcoxon misses here.) .

427.title: Sources
427.content: . | Wilcoxon, F. (1945) “Individual comparisons by ranking methods.” Biometrics Bulletin, 1(6), 80-83 | Python: scipy.stats.wilcoxon(data - threshold, alternative='greater') with meets = p_val >= alpha | .

428.title: 12. Exact Binomial Test
428.content: H-07 - SLA breach rate estimation - Exact probability for rare events .

429.title: Is the Breach Rate Acceptable?
429.content: Given x SLA breaches out of n trials, tests whether the true breach rate is below the target. Uses exact combinatorial probability – no approximation, no assumptions.

430.title: How It Works
430.content: . | Count SLA breaches: runs where metric exceeds threshold | Test H₀: breach_rate ≥ target vs Hₐ: breach_rate < target | Compute exact p-value using binomial distribution | Compute Clopper-Pearson CI for guaranteed coverage | . Key Insight: At n=30, proving breach_rate ≤ 5% requires 0 observed breaches. Even 1/30 = 3.3% observed gives CI upper bound of ~16.7%, which doesn’t exclude 5%.

431.title: In Simple Terms
431.content: Imagine testing a fire extinguisher 30 times. It fails once (3.3% failure rate). Can you guarantee the true failure rate is below 5%? The binomial test says: “With only 30 tests, that one failure creates enough uncertainty that we can’t be sure. You’d need ~93 tests with 0-2 failures to be confident.” .

432.title: Output Example
432.content: SLA breach analysis (TTD > 300s): Application: 1/30 breaches (3.3%) Clopper-Pearson CI: [0.001, 0.167] Binomial test (target 5%): p=0.51 --> INCONCLUSIVE Required n for certification: 59 (if observed rate stays ~3.3%) Network: 8/30 breaches (26.7%) Clopper-Pearson CI: [0.129, 0.446] Binomial test (target 5%): p=0.999 --> FAIL (clearly exceeds target) .

433.title: Sources
433.content: . | Clopper, C.J. & Pearson, E.S. (1934) “The use of confidence or fiducial limits.” Biometrika, 26(4) | Python: scipy.stats.binomtest(x, n, p=target, alternative='less') | .

434.title: 13. TOST (Two One-Sided Tests)
434.content: H-06 - Equivalence testing - Proving performance is within bounds .

435.title: Proving You’re Good Enough (Not Just “Not Bad”)
435.content: Standard tests prove “different from threshold.” TOST proves the opposite: “performance is demonstrably within acceptable bounds.” Used in pharmaceutical bioequivalence – now applied to SLA compliance.

436.title: How It Works
436.content: . | Define equivalence bounds: [SLA_lower, SLA_upper] (e.g., [0, 300s]) | Run TWO one-sided tests: . | Test 1: H₀: μ ≤ lower_bound vs Hₐ: μ > lower_bound | Test 2: H₀: μ ≥ upper_bound vs Hₐ: μ < upper_bound | . | If BOTH reject: metric is provably within bounds | .

437.title: In Simple Terms
437.content: Think of bioequivalence testing for generic drugs. The FDA doesn’t ask “is this drug different?” – they ask “is this drug within 80-125% of the original?” TOST answers the same question for SLAs: “Can we prove the agent is within the acceptable zone?” .

438.title: Output Example
438.content: TOST for time_to_detect (equivalence bounds: [0, 300s]): Application: Test 1 p<0.001, Test 2 p=0.001 --> EQUIVALENT (within bounds) Network: Test 1 p<0.001, Test 2 p=0.218 --> NOT EQUIVALENT (fails upper bound) Resource: Test 1 p<0.001, Test 2 p=0.032 --> EQUIVALENT (borderline) .

439.title: Note on One-Sided Metrics
439.content: For metrics with natural bounds like TTD ∈ [0, SLA] (where negative values are impossible), the lower-bound test (H₀: μ ≤ 0, Hₐ: μ > 0) is trivially true — any positive measurement rejects it. This reduces TOST to a single one-sided t-test against the upper bound, making it the parametric complement of the Wilcoxon signed-rank test (Method 11). TOST remains valuable for its formal power analysis (computing required sample size for a given equivalence margin) and for two-sided metrics where both bounds are non-trivial.

440.title: Sources
440.content: . | Schuirmann, D.J. (1987) “A comparison of the two one-sided tests procedure and the power approach.” J. Pharmacokinetics and Biopharmaceutics, 15(6) | Lakens, D. (2017) “Equivalence Tests.” Social Psychological and Personality Science, 8(4) | Python: statsmodels.stats.weightstats.ttost_ind() or manual implementation | .

441.title: 14. CVaR (Conditional Value-at-Risk)
441.content: H-08 - Tail risk severity - How bad are the worst cases? .

442.title: When P95 Lies
442.content: P95 tells you the 95th percentile – a single threshold. CVaR₉₅ tells you the average of everything beyond P95 – the severity of tail events. An agent with P95=290s but CVaR₉₅=850s has catastrophic hidden risk.

443.title: How It Works
443.content: . | Sort all observations | Find the 95th percentile (VaR₉₅) | Average all values above VaR₉₅ – this is CVaR₉₅ | | With SLA: compute expected overshoot = E[X - SLA | X > SLA] | . | . CVaR₉₅ = average of worst 5% of outcomes Expected overshoot = average(value - SLA_threshold) for values exceeding SLA .

444.title: In Simple Terms
444.content: Imagine a flood insurance assessment. P95 tells you “the worst flood in 20 years reaches 5 feet.” CVaR₉₅ tells you “when floods exceed 5 feet, they average 12 feet.” The first number makes you buy insurance; the second determines how much damage you’ll actually face.

445.title: Output Example
445.content: Tail risk analysis for time_to_detect: Application: P95=278s, CVaR₉₅=287s (mild tail -- worst cases barely exceed P95) Network: P95=845s, CVaR₉₅=891s (catastrophic -- worst 5% avg nearly 15 min!) Resource: P95=512s, CVaR₉₅=539s (moderate tail) With SLA (≤300s): Application: overshoot when >300s: mean=0s (no breaches beyond 300s in sample) Network: overshoot when >300s: mean=462s (breaches avg 762s -- 2.5x SLA!) Resource: overshoot when >300s: mean=134s (breaches modest, avg 434s) .

446.title: Sources
446.content: . | Rockafellar, R.T. & Uryasev, S. (2000) “Optimization of conditional value-at-risk.” Journal of Risk, 2(3) | Artzner, P. et al. (1999) “Coherent measures of risk.” Mathematical Finance, 9(3) | Python: np.mean(sorted_data[int(0.95 * len(data)):]) | .

447.title: 15. Kaplan-Meier Survival Estimator
447.content: H-06 - Time-dependent SLA compliance - Survival analysis .

448.title: What Fraction Are Still “Failing” at Time t?
448.content: Models the probability S(t) = P(TTD > t) – the survival function. At the SLA threshold time, S(SLA) gives the expected breach probability. Handles censored data – runs where detection never occurred within the timeout window.

449.title: How It Works
449.content: . | Sort all detection times | At each time point, compute: S(t) = product of (1 - d_i/n_i) for all events up to t . | d_i = number of detections at time t_i | n_i = number still “at risk” (undetected) at time t_i | . | Runs that timed out without detection are “right-censored” – they contribute to n_i but not d_i | .

450.title: In Simple Terms
450.content: Think of a race timer. You’re tracking how many runners have finished at each minute mark. At the 5-minute mark (SLA), how many are still running? Kaplan-Meier handles the tricky case where some runners dropped out (timed out) – we don’t know if they would have finished eventually.

451.title: Output Example
451.content: Kaplan-Meier at SLA threshold (300s): Application: S(300) = 0.03 (97% detected within SLA time -- excellent) Network: S(300) = 0.73 (only 27% detected within SLA -- most still undetected!) Resource: S(300) = 0.24 (76% detected within SLA -- acceptable) Note: 15 of 30 network runs are right-censored (never detected within 1200s timeout). Kaplan-Meier properly accounts for this, unlike simple rate calculations.

452.title: When to Use Kaplan-Meier
